name: Bohemia
description: The Bohemian basin enclosed by the Sudeten and Ore Mountains — modern Czech Republic. Culturally distinct Slavic zone; seat of the Přemyslid, Luxembourg, and Habsburg dynasties.

periods.note: Celtic then Germanic — Boii (Celtic tribe, gave Bohemia its name) inhabited the basin; displaced by Marcomanni (Germanic) around 10 BCE. The Marcomanni kingdom under Maroboduus briefly rivalled Rome. Bohemia served as a buffer zone — Rome never conquered it.

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant from ~10 BCE (Marcomanni, then other Germanic peoples)

periods.note: Slavic settlement — West Slavic peoples moved into the Bohemian basin during the 6th century Migration Period, filling the vacuum left by Germanic peoples who moved west. The short-lived Samo's Empire (~623–658) was the first Slavic political union. The Great Moravian Empire (833–907) briefly dominated central Europe and received the Slavic missionaries Cyril and Methodius (863), who created the Glagolitic alphabet and translated the Bible into Old Church Slavonic.

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant — West Slavic settlers (ancestors of Czechs and Slovaks)

periods.note: Přemyslid (895–1306) → Luxembourg (1310–1437) → contested (1437–1526). Christianised under Wenceslas I (c.921). Bohemia became an electorate of the HRE but maintained significant autonomy. Charles IV (Luxembourg, r.1346–1378) made Prague the imperial capital — the golden age of Bohemian culture. Jan Hus burned at Constance 1415; the Hussite Wars (1419–1434) were the first successful military defence of a proto-Protestant reform movement.

ethnic_composition.presence: significant urban and mining minority — Sudetenland Germans; German was the language of commerce and the imperial court
significant community in Prague — one of Europe's most important medieval Jewish centres

periods.note: Habsburg Bohemia — Ferdinand I of Habsburg elected King of Bohemia 1526 after Louis II died at Mohács. Bohemian Protestant nobility revolted against Habsburg Catholic rule; the Defenestration of Prague (1618) triggered the Thirty Years' War. Battle of White Mountain (1620) was a catastrophe for Czech Protestantism — the nobility was executed or exiled, land confiscated, Bohemia re-Catholicised. Czech language and culture were suppressed until the 19th-century National Revival.

ethnic_composition.presence: majority but politically suppressed after 1620; Czech National Revival from 1800s
ruling class, urban centres, Sudeten border regions — privileged under Habsburg rule

periods.note: Czechoslovakia (1918–1993) — created from the Habsburg collapse; one of the most prosperous and democratic states in interwar Europe. Sudeten German minority issue exploited by Hitler; Munich Agreement 1938 ceded Sudetenland; full occupation 1939. Communist rule 1948–1989; Prague Spring 1968 crushed by Soviet invasion. Velvet Revolution 1989; Czechoslovakia peacefully dissolved 1993 into Czech Republic and Slovakia.

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant — 3 million Sudeten Germans expelled 1945–1946; homogeneous Czech state created
